story:
The first time I saw Felix, I had already been working for him for months and had heard a chorus of myths. Nicole, my boss, called him the George Clooney of dendrochronology. Daniela, our project manager, swore he swam the lake at lunch and was a perfect gentleman. They competed at coffee to top each other’s stories. I said I wouldn’t mind meeting him. “I hardly think that’s necessary,” Nicole sniffed.

Two weeks later, when both of them were out, the boss sent me to the site with my draft texts for the public information boards. Felix, tanned and helmeted, looked as good as advertised. In his container office he glanced through my folder without interest. “Does the agency employ only women?” he asked. I said we all loved archaeology. “Archaeologists, I mean,” I added. He shut the folder. “You decide. You’re the specialist.” “Specialista,” I said. His phone rang; he listened, face darkening. “I’ve got to go down.”

Beneath the concrete roof of the future car park, we climbed into the damp pit. He handed me his hard hat—“You can’t be down here dressed like that”—and introduced me to the head of the excavation, a squat man with a ponytail. Pumps throbbed, drills screamed. He led me past students scraping a five-thousand-year layer: potsherds, seeds, bones. I picked up a blackened bit of wood. “Keep it in water,” he said. He tugged me out of the path of a digger. “We found a young woman here, under the settlement layers. Maybe she drowned.” His voice made time thin.

When we came up, my sandals were muddy. He still didn’t choose a layout. “You can be pretty obstinate,” he said, taking back his helmet.

At coffee, Nicole sighed, “He complimented my dress.” “The only women he likes are skeletons,” Daniela snapped. The polygrapher grinned: “Then you’re in with a chance.” I asked if he’d said anything about my designs. Nicole waved me off. “How about: You dig history? We dig it up.” Daniela left, stung. Nicole had taken over the project.

A circular arrived: twenty thousand timbers cataloged, ten thousand microsites processed; there’d be a party at the baths. Nicole and Daniela dressed like princesses. I stayed late at the office and reached the lake alone, out of sorts, feeling looked at and not seen. The stilted baths stood over silvery water. I sat on the quay and slipped into a Stone Age reverie: berries, cloth, grain, aching hands, the lake at dusk. I undressed and swam out. The water received me. Near the deck I saw Felix between Nicole and Daniela. Jealousy cut me. I climbed the steps, bare to the stares, took a glass, and toasted him. He raised his. I felt not humiliated but fierce, as if everyone else were just visitors from another era. I put the wine down and dived.

Next day Nicole whispered to Daniela, “He drove me home.” I swallowed tears. At noon an email: Felix said it was a pity I’d only looked in briefly, asked me to dinner, said I had worn the most beautiful dress. Furious, I told him I was too busy to mess around. He never wrote again. Nicole grew sunnier and left at five; soon she appeared with the excavation boss, kissing him in our lobby. “I thought you and Felix—” I said. “Over after your party stunt,” she said. “You were a bit underdressed.”

I didn’t call Felix. Instead I went to the baths at lunch, waiting and not waiting. Rain, gray water, empty decks. I sat where the party had been and thought of the lake dwellers: short winters, illness, wolves, not knowing. Freedom washed through me. What a foolish thing, to wait for a man who’d treated me like a child.

On a drizzly day, the church clocks struck one. A hand touched my shoulder. Felix stood there in trunks, towel over his shoulders, smiling. “I’ve been expecting you,” I said. “Me too,” he said, and we fell into each other’s arms as though we’d been waiting five thousand years.
